title: AP Biology Unit 4 Question Bank v2

style_notes.hard_questions: relevant distractors with minimal recycling, including partially correct College Board-style traps
style_notes.answer_keys: specific explanations for incorrect choices that identify the exact misconception

questions.text: A researcher studies the hormone aldosterone, a small lipid-soluble signaling molecule. To most directly begin a response, the hormone would usually bind to which type of receptor? The researchers focus on the earliest step that would explain the outcome.
choices: a receptor tyrosine kinase, because lipid-soluble hormones must dimerize receptors on the cell surface before entering the nucleus
a ribosome, because translation is the first step in cell signaling for steroid-like molecules
an intracellular receptor, because aldosterone can diffuse through the plasma membrane and then alter gene expression
a ligand-gated ion channel in the membrane, because all hormones must stay outside the cell and open an ion pore first
questions.explain: aldosterone is lipid-soluble, so it can cross the plasma membrane and bind an intracellular receptor that often changes transcription.
choice_explanations: This choice mixes two ideas. Receptor tyrosine kinases are membrane receptors for many extracellular ligands, but lipid-soluble hormones typically do not need a surface receptor in order to enter the cell.
Ribosomes make proteins after signaling has already affected gene expression; they are not receptors that detect hormones.
Correct. aldosterone is lipid-soluble, so it can cross the plasma membrane and bind an intracellular receptor that often changes transcription.
This is tempting because ligand-gated channels are membrane receptors, but small nonpolar hormones do not have to remain outside the cell and do not generally signal by opening ion channels first.

questions.text: The hormone vasopressin is unable to cross the phospholipid bilayer easily. Which mechanism would most likely allow vasopressin to change the activity of a target cell? The researchers focus on the earliest step that would explain the outcome.
choices: replacing membrane phospholipids with the hormone so the cell surface becomes permanently activated
diffusing directly through the membrane and binding DNA without any receptor
binding to a receptor in the plasma membrane that starts an intracellular signaling pathway
binding to a tRNA in the cytosol so the cell immediately translates a response protein
questions.explain: Because vasopressin is polar, it usually binds a membrane receptor and relies on intracellular relay molecules to produce a response.
choice_explanations: This is not how hormone signaling works. Signaling molecules bind receptors; they do not replace large numbers of membrane phospholipids to activate a cell.
This answer ignores the key detail that vasopressin does not cross the phospholipid bilayer easily, so direct diffusion to DNA is unlikely.
Correct. Because vasopressin is polar, it usually binds a membrane receptor and relies on intracellular relay molecules to produce a response.
tRNA participates in translation, not signal reception. The signal must first be detected by a receptor.

This is not how hormone signaling works. Signaling molecules bind receptors; they do not replace large numbers of membrane phospholipids to activate a cell.
tRNA participates in translation, not signal reception. The signal must first be detected by a receptor.
This answer ignores the key detail that insulin does not cross the phospholipid bilayer easily, so direct diffusion to DNA is unlikely.

questions.text: The hormone growth hormone is unable to cross the phospholipid bilayer easily. Which mechanism would most likely allow growth hormone to change the activity of a target cell? Only one component of the pathway is altered in this scenario.
choices: binding to a receptor in the plasma membrane that starts an intracellular signaling pathway
replacing membrane phospholipids with the hormone so the cell surface becomes permanently activated
binding to a tRNA in the cytosol so the cell immediately translates a response protein
diffusing directly through the membrane and binding DNA without any receptor

questions.text: A mutation allows a G protein-coupled receptor to bind glucagon normally, but the receptor can no longer activate its G protein. Which outcome is most likely? The researchers focus on the earliest step that would explain the outcome.
choices: The signal will be received at the membrane, but downstream relay steps that depend on the G protein will be greatly reduced.
The pathway will switch automatically into a receptor tyrosine kinase pathway because all membrane receptors are interchangeable.
The response will become stronger because the inactive G protein leaves more room for second messengers to diffuse.
The ligand will diffuse through the membrane and activate transcription directly, bypassing the receptor entirely.
questions.explain: Ligand binding still occurs, but the receptor cannot pass the signal to the G protein, so downstream transduction is impaired.
choice_explanations: Correct. Ligand binding still occurs, but the receptor cannot pass the signal to the G protein, so downstream transduction is impaired.
Different receptor classes are not interchangeable. A GPCR defect does not automatically convert the receptor into a receptor tyrosine kinase.
This sounds mechanistic, but second messengers are usually produced downstream of G protein activation. If the G protein is not activated, the pathway is usually weaker, not stronger.
This ignores the fact that the ligand is using a GPCR because it normally requires a membrane receptor. Most such ligands do not simply cross the membrane and directly regulate transcription.

questions.text: Cells are exposed to NGF, which normally binds a receptor tyrosine kinase. A mutant receptor can bind the ligand but cannot dimerize. Which result is most likely? The researchers focus on the earliest step that would explain the outcome.
choices: The pathway will strengthen because one receptor can now phosphorylate itself without any partner, making signaling constitutive.
The ligand will be broken down into cAMP, which replaces the need for receptor phosphorylation.
The receptor will open a membrane pore instead, because undimerized receptor tyrosine kinases convert into ion channels.
The receptor will fail to autophosphorylate efficiently, so downstream signaling will be greatly reduced.
questions.explain: Dimerization is a key step for many receptor tyrosine kinases because it allows autophosphorylation and recruitment of relay proteins.
choice_explanations: Some receptors can signal abnormally when mutated, but the question specifically says the receptor cannot dimerize, which usually prevents the normal autophosphorylation step rather than strengthening it.
Ligands are not generally converted into cAMP. cAMP is a second messenger made inside the cell by enzymes such as adenylyl cyclase.
This answer confuses receptor classes. Failure to dimerize does not turn a receptor tyrosine kinase into a ligand-gated ion channel.
Correct. Dimerization is a key step for many receptor tyrosine kinases because it allows autophosphorylation and recruitment of relay proteins.

questions.text: Cells are exposed to EGF, which normally binds a receptor tyrosine kinase. A mutant receptor can bind the ligand but cannot dimerize. Which result is most likely? No changes in DNA sequence are detected outside the gene or pathway component named.
choices: The receptor will open a membrane pore instead, because undimerized receptor tyrosine kinases convert into ion channels.
The receptor will fail to autophosphorylate efficiently, so downstream signaling will be greatly reduced.
The pathway will strengthen because one receptor can now phosphorylate itself without any partner, making signaling constitutive.
The ligand will be broken down into cAMP, which replaces the need for receptor phosphorylation.

questions.text: Which statement best explains why a small amount of extracellular signal molecule can produce a large cellular response? The researchers focus on the earliest step that would explain the outcome.
choices: One activated receptor can trigger activation of many intracellular molecules, creating signal amplification.
The signal molecule is copied directly by DNA polymerase, which multiplies the signal in the nucleus.
Second messengers are larger than the original ligand, so they automatically contain more energy and therefore a bigger response.
The plasma membrane becomes permanently more permeable to every substance once any receptor is activated.
questions.explain: Amplification occurs because each activated component in a pathway can influence many molecules downstream.
choice_explanations: Correct. Amplification occurs because each activated component in a pathway can influence many molecules downstream.
DNA polymerase replicates DNA during S phase; it does not copy extracellular signals to amplify cell communication.

This distractor is partly tempting because second messengers often participate in amplification, but the reason is not their size or stored energy. The key is that one signal can activate many downstream targets.
This is too broad and biologically inaccurate. Signaling pathways are usually regulated and specific, not a permanent global increase in membrane permeability.

questions.text: The same signaling molecule, epinephrine, causes one response in a skin cell but a different response in a liver cell. Which explanation is most likely? The researchers focus on the earliest step that would explain the outcome.
choices: The second cell must lack a nucleus, so it can only produce a different response.
Once a ligand binds one cell type, it permanently changes its chemical identity before reaching the second cell type.
A signaling molecule always carries multiple different amino acid sequences, and each cell randomly chooses one to read.
The two cell types differ in the receptors or intracellular signaling proteins they express.
questions.explain: Different receptor types or downstream signaling proteins can cause the same signal to produce different responses in different cells.
choice_explanations: Nuclear presence alone does not explain why two cells respond differently to the same signal. The key difference is receptor and pathway specificity.
A ligand is not usually transformed into a completely different signal simply because it bound a cell elsewhere. The response difference is typically due to the target cells.
Signaling molecules do not work because cells read different amino acid sequences from the same ligand in that way. Specific cellular components determine the response.
Correct. Different receptor types or downstream signaling proteins can cause the same signal to produce different responses in different cells.

questions.text: Which type of cell signaling is best illustrated by the following description: a neuron releases neurotransmitter across a synaptic cleft to a nearby target cell? The researchers focus on the earliest step that would explain the outcome.
choices: autocrine signaling
paracrine signaling
endocrine signaling
synaptic signaling
questions.explain: Synaptic signaling is local communication from a neuron across a synapse, not long-distance hormone release through the bloodstream.
choice_explanations: Autocrine signaling affects the same cell that secreted the signal, which is not what the description shows.
Paracrine signaling acts on nearby cells in the same local environment, not on distant targets via circulation.
The signal travels through circulatory fluid to distant target cells, which is characteristic of endocrine signaling.

questions.text: Which statement best classifies the following regulation pattern: platelet activation at a wound site causes more platelets to be recruited to the same site? The researchers focus on the earliest step that would explain the outcome.
choices: signal amplification
feedforward regulation
competitive inhibition
positive feedback
questions.explain: In this scenario, the response reinforces the original change, which defines positive feedback.
choice_explanations: Amplification means one signal activates many downstream molecules. This scenario is about system-level regulation over time, not merely multiplying molecules in a pathway.
Feedforward regulation can prepare a system for a predicted change, but the key pattern here is whether the response opposes or reinforces the original change.
Competitive inhibition describes an enzyme-level mechanism involving an inhibitor and an active site, not this whole-body or whole-system regulatory loop.
Correct. In this scenario, the response reinforces the original change, which defines positive feedback.

questions.text: Which statement best classifies the following regulation pattern: stretching of the cervix during labor triggers oxytocin release, which intensifies contractions? The best answer should explain the pattern without adding an extra unmentioned mutation.
choices: competitive inhibition
signal amplification
positive feedback
feedforward regulation
questions.explain: In this scenario, the response reinforces the original change, which defines positive feedback.

questions.text: A culture of animal cells divides rapidly only when EGF is added to the medium. Which conclusion is best supported? The researchers focus on the earliest step that would explain the outcome.

The cells rely on an external growth factor signal to pass a cell-cycle control point and divide efficiently.
questions.explain: Many animal cells require external growth factor signals to proceed through the cell cycle, especially past the G1 checkpoint.
choice_explanations: This reverses the idea. Normal cells often do respond to growth factors. Cancer cells are notable because they may divide without normal external control.

questions.text: A mutation prevents normal activation of cyclin E during late G1. Which result is most likely in an otherwise healthy cell? The researchers focus on the earliest step that would explain the outcome.
choices: DNA repair will become unnecessary, because late-G1 cyclins replace checkpoint proteins and remove all damage automatically.
The cell will skip cytokinesis but continue entering mitosis normally, because G1 proteins act only at the end of M phase.
The cell will immediately separate sister chromatids, because G1 cyclins directly pull chromosomes apart.
The cell will have difficulty passing the G1 checkpoint and entering S phase.
questions.explain: Late-G1 cyclin/Cdk activity helps commit the cell to DNA replication, so reduced activation commonly delays or blocks entry into S phase.
choice_explanations: Cyclins help regulate cell-cycle progression, but they do not make DNA repair unnecessary or replace all checkpoint functions.
This answer misplaces the role of G1 regulators. Their main effect is before DNA replication, not specifically at the end of mitosis.
Sister chromatids separate during anaphase, and G1 cyclins do not physically pull chromosomes apart.
Correct. Late-G1 cyclin/Cdk activity helps commit the cell to DNA replication, so reduced activation commonly delays or blocks entry into S phase.

The cell will immediately separate sister chromatids, because G1 cyclins directly pull chromosomes apart.
DNA repair will become unnecessary, because late-G1 cyclins replace checkpoint proteins and remove all damage automatically.
questions.explain: Late-G1 cyclin/Cdk activity helps commit the cell to DNA replication, so reduced activation commonly delays or blocks entry into S phase.
choice_explanations: This answer misplaces the role of G1 regulators. Their main effect is before DNA replication, not specifically at the end of mitosis.
Correct. Late-G1 cyclin/Cdk activity helps commit the cell to DNA replication, so reduced activation commonly delays or blocks entry into S phase.
Sister chromatids separate during anaphase, and G1 cyclins do not physically pull chromosomes apart.
Cyclins help regulate cell-cycle progression, but they do not make DNA repair unnecessary or replace all checkpoint functions.

questions.text: A cell experiences extensive DNA damage just before entering S phase. If p53 is functioning normally, what is the most likely immediate outcome? The researchers focus on the earliest step that would explain the outcome.
choices: The cell will bypass all checkpoints because tumor suppressors exist mainly to speed tissue growth after injury.
The cell cycle will pause or stop so the damage can be repaired, and severe damage may lead to apoptosis.
The cell will accelerate into S phase so damaged DNA can be copied before repair enzymes remove it.
p53 will convert directly into a growth factor and stimulate neighboring cells to divide in compensation.
questions.explain: p53 is a tumor suppressor that can arrest the cell cycle after DNA damage and, if the damage is too severe, help trigger apoptosis.
choice_explanations: Tumor suppressors do not normally bypass checkpoints; they restrain progression when conditions are unsafe.
Correct. p53 is a tumor suppressor that can arrest the cell cycle after DNA damage and, if the damage is too severe, help trigger apoptosis.
This is the opposite of normal p53 function. p53 helps prevent damaged DNA from being replicated.
p53 is a regulatory protein involved in damage response, not a secreted growth factor that stimulates neighboring cells.

questions.text: A researcher adds a spindle poison to dividing cells. Many cells arrest with chromosomes aligned near the middle of the cell but not all kinetochores attached correctly. Which checkpoint is most directly responsible for the arrest? The researchers focus on the earliest step that would explain the outcome.
choices: G2 checkpoint
S-phase checkpoint
G1 checkpoint
M checkpoint
questions.explain: The spindle assembly checkpoint in M phase prevents anaphase until chromosomes are properly attached to spindle fibers.
choice_explanations: The G2 checkpoint checks whether DNA replication is complete before mitosis, not whether kinetochores are attached during metaphase.
DNA replication control during S phase is not the most direct checkpoint monitoring spindle attachment in mitosis.
The G1 checkpoint acts before DNA replication, not during chromosome attachment in mitosis.
Correct. The spindle assembly checkpoint in M phase prevents anaphase until chromosomes are properly attached to spindle fibers.

questions.text: Which phase of mitosis is best identified by the event: duplicated chromosomes line up at the metaphase plate? The researchers focus on the earliest step that would explain the outcome.
choices: metaphase
prometaphase
anaphase
prophase
questions.explain: Duplicated chromosomes line up at the metaphase plate is the defining event of metaphase.
choice_explanations: Correct. Duplicated chromosomes line up at the metaphase plate is the defining event of metaphase.
Prometaphase is tempting because it occurs near this stage in mitosis, but its defining event is different: the nuclear envelope fragments and spindle microtubules attach to kinetochores.
Anaphase is tempting because it occurs near this stage in mitosis, but its defining event is different: sister chromatids separate and move toward opposite poles.
Prophase is tempting because it occurs near this stage in mitosis, but its defining event is different: chromosomes condense and the mitotic spindle begins to form.

questions.text: Which phase of mitosis is best identified by the event: nuclear envelopes reform around the separated chromosome sets? The result is measured within minutes, before new proteins are made.
choices: prophase
metaphase

questions.text: A population of cultured animal cells stops dividing when the dish becomes crowded, but resumes dividing after some cells are removed. Which property best explains this behavior? The researchers focus on the earliest step that would explain the outcome.
choices: density-dependent inhibition
metastasis
binary fission
positive feedback
questions.explain: Many normal animal cells reduce division when they become crowded and contact many neighboring cells.
choice_explanations: Correct. Many normal animal cells reduce division when they become crowded and contact many neighboring cells.
Metastasis is the spread of malignant cancer cells, not the normal slowing of division in a crowded culture.
Binary fission is the typical division mechanism of prokaryotes, not the crowding response shown by these animal cells.
Feedback language sounds plausible, but the specific property of normal animal cells responding to crowding is density-dependent inhibition.

questions.text: Which statement best describes anchorage dependence in many animal cells? The researchers focus on the earliest step that would explain the outcome.
choices: The cells usually must be attached to a surface or extracellular matrix to divide normally.
The cells must anchor their chromosomes to the nuclear envelope before any gene can be expressed.
The cells need to anchor growth factors permanently into the membrane so receptors never turn off.
The cells can divide only if suspended freely in fluid, because contact with surfaces suppresses all signaling.
questions.explain: Many noncancerous animal cells require attachment to a substratum or extracellular matrix for normal division.
choice_explanations: Correct. Many noncancerous animal cells require attachment to a substratum or extracellular matrix for normal division.
This confuses cell attachment with nuclear organization. Anchorage dependence refers to attachment to a surface, not chromosome anchoring before gene expression.
Growth factors can bind receptors, but anchorage dependence is about physical attachment to a surface or matrix, not permanently fixing growth factors in the membrane.
This is the opposite of anchorage dependence. Many normal animal cells divide poorly when not attached.

questions.text: A mutation causes the normal growth-promoting gene HER2 to signal for division even when no growth factor is present. The altered gene is best classified as what? The researchers focus on the earliest step that would explain the outcome.
choices: an oncogene
a caspase
a tumor suppressor gene
a checkpoint
questions.explain: A growth-promoting gene that becomes overactive and drives cell division inappropriately is an oncogene.
choice_explanations: Correct. A growth-promoting gene that becomes overactive and drives cell division inappropriately is an oncogene.
Caspases execute apoptosis and are not the usual category for an overactive growth-promoting gene.
Tumor suppressor genes usually restrain division or promote repair/apoptosis; the scenario describes a gene whose activity is now excessively pro-growth.
A checkpoint is a control point in the cell cycle, not the classification of a mutated gene.

questions.text: Which mutation would most directly increase the risk of uncontrolled cell division by removing a normal brake on the cell cycle? The researchers focus on the earliest step that would explain the outcome.
choices: loss of function in an enzyme that adds cellulose to plant cell walls
a change that lowers ATP use by sodium-potassium pumps in resting cells
a mutation that makes a lysosomal enzyme slightly less active during digestion of worn-out organelles
loss of function in a tumor suppressor gene such as p53 or Rb
questions.explain: Tumor suppressor genes normally restrain the cell cycle or promote repair/apoptosis, so losing them removes an important brake on division.
choice_explanations: This could affect cell structure in plants, but it is not the most direct explanation for uncontrolled division in the cell-cycle regulation context.

This could affect recycling, but it is not the classic direct route to removing a checkpoint or cell-cycle brake.
Correct. Tumor suppressor genes normally restrain the cell cycle or promote repair/apoptosis, so losing them removes an important brake on division.

questions.text: A signaling pathway causes a cell to activate caspases, fragment its DNA, and shrink in an orderly way without bursting. Which process is occurring? The researchers focus on the earliest step that would explain the outcome.
choices: necrosis triggered by osmotic lysis
binary fission
apoptosis
metastasis
questions.explain: Orderly cell shrinkage, DNA fragmentation, and caspase activation are hallmarks of apoptosis.
choice_explanations: Necrosis often involves loss of membrane integrity and swelling or rupture, whereas the prompt describes an orderly, programmed process.
Binary fission is a mode of prokaryotic cell division, not programmed cell death.
Correct. Orderly cell shrinkage, DNA fragmentation, and caspase activation are hallmarks of apoptosis.
Metastasis is the spread of cancer cells to new locations, not the death of a cell via a caspase-driven pathway.

questions.text: Plant and animal cells complete cytokinesis differently. Which statement is most accurate? The researchers focus on the earliest step that would explain the outcome.
choices: Both plant and animal cells divide the cytoplasm by dissolving the plasma membrane entirely and rebuilding it after mitosis.
Cytokinesis occurs only in prokaryotes, whereas eukaryotes complete division at telophase without separating cytoplasm.
Plant cells use a cleavage furrow because their cell wall contracts inward, while animal cells assemble a cell plate from cellulose vesicles.
Animal cells commonly form a cleavage furrow, whereas plant cells build a cell plate that develops into a new wall between daughter cells.
questions.explain: Animal cytokinesis uses a contractile ring and cleavage furrow, while plant cytokinesis forms a cell plate.
choice_explanations: Cells do not normally dissolve the entire plasma membrane to complete cytokinesis. The mechanisms are more controlled and structurally distinct.
Eukaryotic cells do undergo cytokinesis. Telophase concerns nuclear reformation, not the complete separation of the cytoplasm by itself.
This reverses the two mechanisms. The rigid plant wall prevents a typical cleavage furrow; plant cells form a cell plate instead.
Correct. Animal cytokinesis uses a contractile ring and cleavage furrow, while plant cytokinesis forms a cell plate.

questions.text: A epinephrine binds to a receptor and activates a transcription factor in one cell type but opens ion channels in another. Which conclusion is best supported? The researchers focus on the earliest step that would explain the outcome.
choices: Only cells in S phase can respond through transcription factors, whereas all other cells can respond only through ion channels.
The two cells must have identical signaling proteins, so the difference can only come from different DNA sequences in the ligand.
A signal always has only one possible response, so one of the observations must be experimental error.
The same signal can produce different responses because target cells may have different receptors or downstream proteins.
questions.explain: Different receptor types and intracellular machinery allow one signal to produce different responses in different cells.
choice_explanations: Cell-cycle phase does not create such an absolute division of signaling outcomes. Cells in many phases can regulate transcription or ion channels depending on the pathway.
The ligand is the same signal; the important variable is the target cell's receptor and pathway components, not different DNA sequences in the ligand.

questions.text: Researchers label insulin with a fluorescent tag and add it to two groups of cells. Group 1 expresses the normal receptor for insulin. Group 2 expresses a mutant receptor missing the ligand-binding domain. After 5 minutes, fluorescence is strong on the membrane of Group 1 but nearly absent in Group 2. Which conclusion is best supported? All other conditions are kept constant between groups.
choices: The ligand-binding domain is needed only for transduction, not for ligand attachment, so the fluorescence pattern does not matter.
The mutant receptor must still trigger a stronger response, because receptors without a binding domain are constitutively activated.
The missing ligand-binding domain prevents normal reception of the signal, so the mutant receptor cannot bind the ligand effectively.
Group 2 likely imported the ligand into the nucleus too quickly to detect it on the membrane.
questions.explain: If fluorescence marks ligand binding and the mutant lacks the ligand-binding domain, weak fluorescence supports failed reception.
choice_explanations: This is partly tempting because domains can affect signaling, but a ligand-binding domain is directly involved in attachment of the signal molecule, which is exactly what the fluorescence assay measures.
Some mutations can cause constitutive signaling, but the data given specifically address binding, and weak fluorescence supports reduced ligand binding rather than stronger reception.
Correct. If fluorescence marks ligand binding and the mutant lacks the ligand-binding domain, weak fluorescence supports failed reception.
This distractor tries to explain away the missing membrane signal, but a receptor missing its binding domain would not be expected to capture the ligand efficiently in the first place.

Group 2 expresses a mutant receptor missing the ligand-binding domain. After 5 minutes, fluorescence is strong on the membrane of Group 1 but nearly absent in Group 2. Which conclusion is best supported? The same instrument settings are used for all samples.
choices: The missing ligand-binding domain prevents normal reception of the signal, so the mutant receptor cannot bind the ligand effectively.
The ligand-binding domain is needed only for transduction, not for ligand attachment, so the fluorescence pattern does not matter.
The mutant receptor must still trigger a stronger response, because receptors without a binding domain are constitutively activated.
Group 2 likely imported the ligand into the nucleus too quickly to detect it on the membrane.
questions.explain: If fluorescence marks ligand binding and the mutant lacks the ligand-binding domain, weak fluorescence supports failed reception.
choice_explanations: Correct. If fluorescence marks ligand binding and the mutant lacks the ligand-binding domain, weak fluorescence supports failed reception.

This is partly tempting because domains can affect signaling, but a ligand-binding domain is directly involved in attachment of the signal molecule, which is exactly what the fluorescence assay measures.
Some mutations can cause constitutive signaling, but the data given specifically address binding, and weak fluorescence supports reduced ligand binding rather than stronger reception.
This distractor tries to explain away the missing membrane signal, but a receptor missing its binding domain would not be expected to capture the ligand efficiently in the first place.

questions.text: Scientists treat liver cells with epinephrine. In one group, they also add an adenylyl cyclase inhibitor. After 2 minutes, the control group shows a large increase in cAMP, whereas the inhibitor group shows almost no increase. Which interpretation is best supported? All other conditions are kept constant between groups.
choices: The receptor for the hormone must be an intracellular receptor, because cAMP levels changed in the cytoplasm.
Adenylyl cyclase is required in this pathway to generate cAMP downstream of receptor activation.
Because cAMP increased in the control, the inhibitor must have blocked translation rather than signaling.
The hormone itself was probably converted directly into cAMP by the receptor.
questions.explain: The inhibitor prevented the rise in cAMP, so adenylyl cyclase is needed to produce cAMP in response to the signal.
choice_explanations: cAMP is an intracellular second messenger, but its presence does not mean the receptor is intracellular. In fact, cAMP commonly acts downstream of membrane receptors such as GPCRs.
Correct. The inhibitor prevented the rise in cAMP, so adenylyl cyclase is needed to produce cAMP in response to the signal.
The experiment measures a second messenger only minutes after treatment. That timescale fits signaling much better than blocking translation.
The signal molecule is not typically converted into cAMP. Instead, receptor activation stimulates an enzyme that synthesizes cAMP inside the cell.

questions.text: In cultured cells, a local signal normally causes a sharp rise in cytosolic Ca2+. Researchers repeat the experiment after adding a drug that blocks Ca2+ release channels in the endoplasmic reticulum. The Ca2+ spike becomes much smaller. Which conclusion is best supported? All other conditions are kept constant between groups.
choices: The original signal must have entered the nucleus and bound DNA directly, because calcium is no longer involved.
Internal stores in the endoplasmic reticulum contribute substantially to the calcium signal.
All calcium in signal transduction must come from outside the cell, so the reduced spike proves the drug blocked membrane transporters only.
The ER release channels normally degrade the signaling molecule into phosphate groups, so blocking them removes phosphorylation.
questions.explain: Blocking ER calcium release reduced the cytosolic Ca2+ spike, showing that internal stores are part of the response.
choice_explanations: Calcium is still involved; the experiment shows the calcium increase was reduced, not that DNA binding explains the response.
Correct. Blocking ER calcium release reduced the cytosolic Ca2+ spike, showing that internal stores are part of the response.
The result points the other way: blocking ER channels reduced the signal, showing that intracellular stores contribute. Not all signaling calcium must come from outside the cell.
This choice confuses calcium channels with enzymes involved in phosphorylation. The experiment specifically concerns Ca2+ release from the ER.

questions.text: In cultured cells, a neurotransmitter normally causes a sharp rise in cytosolic Ca2+. Researchers repeat the experiment after adding a drug that blocks Ca2+ release channels in the endoplasmic reticulum. The Ca2+ spike becomes much smaller. Which conclusion is best supported? The researcher is trying to identify the most direct explanation for the pattern.
choices: All calcium in signal transduction must come from outside the cell, so the reduced spike proves the drug blocked membrane transporters only.
The original signal must have entered the nucleus and bound DNA directly, because calcium is no longer involved.
The ER release channels normally degrade the signaling molecule into phosphate groups, so blocking them removes phosphorylation.
Internal stores in the endoplasmic reticulum contribute substantially to the calcium signal.

questions.text: Researchers expose cells to FGF and run a Western blot using an antibody that detects phosphorylated tyrosine residues on the receptor. Untreated cells show no band, but treated cells show a strong phosphorylated-receptor band within 1 minute. Which interpretation is best supported? All other conditions are kept constant between groups.
choices: The cells entered S phase immediately, because receptor phosphorylation always means DNA replication has already begun.
Ligand binding activated a receptor tyrosine kinase, leading to receptor phosphorylation early in the pathway.
The receptor must be a ligand-gated ion channel, because only ion channels can change state within 1 minute.
The growth factor was translated by ribosomes into a phosphatase that removed phosphate groups from the receptor.
questions.explain: A rapid increase in phosphorylated tyrosines on the receptor is classic evidence of receptor tyrosine kinase activation.
choice_explanations: Receptor phosphorylation can be an early signaling event long before DNA replication. It does not mean the cells have already entered S phase.
Correct. A rapid increase in phosphorylated tyrosines on the receptor is classic evidence of receptor tyrosine kinase activation.
Ion channels can respond quickly, but the key data are receptor tyrosine phosphorylation, which specifically supports kinase-type receptor activation.
This contradicts the observed increase in receptor phosphorylation and incorrectly treats the extracellular signal as something translated by ribosomes.

questions.text: acetylcholine is added to a culture containing muscle cells and pancreatic cells. Within minutes, the muscle cells increase cAMP levels, while the pancreatic cells show no cAMP change but begin expressing an immediate-response gene. Which conclusion is most reasonable? All other conditions are kept constant between groups.
choices: Only cells with cAMP responses are true target cells, so the gene-expression response in the other cells must be contamination.
The cells with the gene-expression response cannot contain receptors because receptors only work on second messengers, not transcription.
The two cell types likely use different receptors or downstream signaling proteins, so the same signal produces different responses.
The ligand must have changed its chemical identity between one cell type and the other.
questions.explain: Different target cells can respond differently to the same ligand because they do not necessarily share the same receptors or transduction pathways.
choice_explanations: A target-cell response does not have to involve cAMP. Gene-expression changes can be a legitimate response through a different pathway.
This choice is partly right that receptors can act through second messengers, but it is wrong because receptors can also activate pathways that ultimately change transcription.
Correct. Different target cells can respond differently to the same ligand because they do not necessarily share the same receptors or transduction pathways.
A single signal can trigger different responses without changing identity. The more likely explanation is that the target cells differ.

questions.text: Animal cells are plated at equal density in three dishes. Dish A receives complete medium. Dish B receives medium lacking serum and growth factors. Dish C receives the same serum-free medium as Dish B plus purified EGF. After 24 hours, cell counts rise strongly in A and C but barely change in B. Which conclusion is best supported? All other conditions are kept constant between groups.
choices: The added growth factor can replace a missing external proliferation signal and help the cells continue through the cell cycle.
Serum-free cells in Dish B failed to divide because DNA polymerase is found only in serum.
The growth factor most likely served as a structural component of the spindle fibers, allowing mitosis to occur in Dish C.
Because Dish C grew, the cells in Dish C must have become permanently cancerous during the experiment.
questions.explain: Because Dish C recovered growth while Dish B did not, the purified growth factor can substitute for an external signal needed for proliferation.
choice_explanations: Correct. Because Dish C recovered growth while Dish B did not, the purified growth factor can substitute for an external signal needed for proliferation.
Serum contains signaling molecules and nutrients, but DNA polymerase is produced by the cells themselves and is not simply supplied by serum.
Growth factors signal through receptors and pathways; they are not structural subunits of spindle microtubules.
Responding to a normal growth factor does not mean the cells became cancerous. Normal animal cells often require such signals to divide.

The growth factor most likely served as a structural component of the spindle fibers, allowing mitosis to occur in Dish C.
Serum-free cells in Dish B failed to divide because DNA polymerase is found only in serum.
questions.explain: Because Dish C recovered growth while Dish B did not, the purified growth factor can substitute for an external signal needed for proliferation.
choice_explanations: Correct. Because Dish C recovered growth while Dish B did not, the purified growth factor can substitute for an external signal needed for proliferation.
Responding to a normal growth factor does not mean the cells became cancerous. Normal animal cells often require such signals to divide.
Growth factors signal through receptors and pathways; they are not structural subunits of spindle microtubules.
Serum contains signaling molecules and nutrients, but DNA polymerase is produced by the cells themselves and is not simply supplied by serum.

questions.text: Researchers grow normal fibroblasts to confluence in a culture dish so that the cells touch one another on all sides. They then scratch a narrow line through the cell layer, creating an empty gap. Over the next day, cells at the edge of the gap begin dividing and moving into the open space. Which property is best illustrated by this result? All other conditions are kept constant between groups.
choices: anchorage independence, because cells attached to the dish are unable to sense neighboring cells
density-dependent inhibition, because contact with neighboring cells suppresses division until open space becomes available
binary fission, because the cells split in response to damage in the monolayer
positive feedback, because each cell division event forces all neighboring cells to divide more rapidly forever
questions.explain: The edge cells resume dividing when the crowding signal is relieved, which illustrates density-dependent inhibition.
choice_explanations: The cells are attached to the dish in both conditions. The key difference is the degree of crowding, which points to density-dependent inhibition rather than anchorage independence.
Correct. The edge cells resume dividing when the crowding signal is relieved, which illustrates density-dependent inhibition.
Binary fission is a prokaryotic division mechanism, not the explanation for how animal fibroblasts respond to changes in crowding.
Some signaling loops can reinforce themselves, but the specific phenomenon shown here is release from crowding-related growth suppression, not runaway positive feedback.

questions.text: Two cell lines are mixed separately into soft agar, which does not provide a solid surface for normal attachment. Line 1 forms almost no colonies. Line 2 forms many colonies throughout the agar. Which interpretation is best supported? All other conditions are kept constant between groups.
choices: Line 2 must be dividing by binary fission because it is not attached to a surface.
Line 1 is more likely to be cancerous because it requires attachment signals before dividing.
Line 2 shows reduced anchorage dependence, a trait often associated with transformed or cancer-like cells.
Soft agar prevents all signaling, so any growth in Line 2 must come from photosynthesis rather than cell-cycle control.
questions.explain: Growth without normal surface attachment suggests loss of anchorage dependence, which is common in transformed cells.
choice_explanations: Binary fission is a prokaryotic process and is unrelated to whether an animal cell line is attached to a surface.
This reverses the usual interpretation. Normal animal cells commonly require attachment, whereas cancer-like cells may grow without it.
Correct. Growth without normal surface attachment suggests loss of anchorage dependence, which is common in transformed cells.
Cells in soft agar can still signal. Growth there is interpreted in terms of attachment requirements, not photosynthesis.

questions.text: A scientist analyzes DNA content in a cell population by flow cytometry. In untreated cells, many cells show intermediate DNA content typical of S phase, and a smaller group shows doubled DNA content typical of G2/M. After adding a drug that prevents cells from passing the G1 checkpoint, most cells accumulate with the lower DNA content characteristic of G1. Which conclusion is best supported? All other conditions are kept constant between groups.
choices: The drug causes chromosomes to separate too early during anaphase, which lowers DNA content below G1 levels.
The drug blocks progression before DNA replication begins, so cells remain in G1 instead of entering S phase.
Because some cells still have doubled DNA content, the G1 checkpoint must occur after mitosis rather than before S phase.
The drug directly increases cAMP, and cAMP always prevents DNA replication regardless of pathway context.
questions.explain: Accumulation at the lower DNA-content peak indicates that cells are not progressing into DNA replication.
choice_explanations: The data show accumulation in G1, not premature chromatid separation or DNA loss below the normal G1 amount.
Correct. Accumulation at the lower DNA-content peak indicates that cells are not progressing into DNA replication.
The presence of cells already beyond G1 does not change the checkpoint's position. The key point is that new cells accumulate before S phase begins.
cAMP can regulate many pathways, but the evidence given is specifically about arrest before DNA replication; the question does not support the claim that cAMP is the mechanism.

questions.text: Cells with normal p53 and cells lacking functional p53 are both exposed to ultraviolet radiation that damages DNA. Six hours later, the normal-p53 cells show increased expression of DNA-repair genes and a higher fraction of apoptotic cells when damage is severe. The p53-deficient cells continue dividing more often. Which conclusion is best supported? All other conditions are kept constant between groups.
choices: Apoptosis in the normal cells proves that ultraviolet light destroys all signaling receptors before DNA repair can begin.
p53 is a growth factor secreted by damaged cells to stimulate neighbors to repair DNA.
p53 normally helps stop the cell cycle after DNA damage and can promote apoptosis when the damage is extensive.
Cells lacking p53 repair DNA faster because they skip the checkpoint that slows replication.
questions.explain: The contrast between the two cell types shows p53 functioning as a damage-response regulator that can arrest the cell cycle and help trigger apoptosis.
choice_explanations: The experiment points specifically to DNA-damage response and apoptosis regulation, not wholesale destruction of receptors.
p53 is an intracellular tumor suppressor protein, not a secreted growth factor used for signaling neighboring cells.
Correct. The contrast between the two cell types shows p53 functioning as a damage-response regulator that can arrest the cell cycle and help trigger apoptosis.
They may continue dividing more often, but that does not mean they repair DNA faster. In fact, skipping a checkpoint can allow damaged DNA to persist.

questions.text: A class observes onion root tip cells under a microscope. One group of roots is untreated. Another group is treated with colchicine, a drug that interferes with microtubule formation. Compared with the control roots, the treated roots show many more cells with condensed chromosomes that are not separating into two groups. Which interpretation is best supported? All other conditions are kept constant between groups.
choices: Disrupting microtubules prevents proper spindle function, so cells accumulate before or at metaphase instead of completing chromosome separation.
Colchicine increases DNA replication, so the chromosomes become too heavy to separate during cytokinesis.
The treated cells bypass the M checkpoint and therefore move through mitosis faster than the control cells.
Microtubules are used only in cytokinesis, so chromosome separation should be unaffected by colchicine.
questions.explain: Without functional spindle microtubules, chromosomes cannot be properly moved apart, so cells tend to arrest around metaphase.
choice_explanations: Correct. Without functional spindle microtubules, chromosomes cannot be properly moved apart, so cells tend to arrest around metaphase.
The problem is spindle function, not heavier chromosomes from increased DNA replication.
The observation of many cells stuck with condensed chromosomes indicates delay or arrest, not faster progression through mitosis.
Microtubules are crucial for the mitotic spindle and chromosome movement, not only cytokinesis.

questions.text: Researchers synchronize a cell population so that most cells begin the cell cycle at the same time. They sample proteins every 30 minutes. Cyclin B is low early, rises steadily during interphase, peaks just before mitosis, and then drops sharply as cells exit mitosis. Which interpretation best explains the sharp drop? All other conditions are kept constant between groups.
choices: Cyclin B is secreted as a hormone to neighboring cells after mitosis, which is why its intracellular level drops.
Cyclin B becomes DNA and is incorporated into chromosomes after metaphase, so its protein level falls.
Cyclin B is degraded as cells exit mitosis, helping turn off the cyclin-Cdk activity that drove entry into M phase.
Its decline proves the cells skipped S phase and therefore could not maintain any proteins by the end of mitosis.
questions.explain: Cyclin levels rise and fall because timed synthesis and degradation regulate cyclin-Cdk activity across the cell cycle.
choice_explanations: Cyclin B is a cell-cycle regulatory protein within the cell, not a hormone secreted to signal neighboring cells.
Cyclin proteins regulate the cell cycle; they are not converted into DNA and added to chromosomes.
Correct. Cyclin levels rise and fall because timed synthesis and degradation regulate cyclin-Cdk activity across the cell cycle.
The pattern described is a normal rise-and-fall cycle of cyclin regulation, not evidence that S phase was skipped.

questions.text: An inhibitor that prevents actin filaments from contracting is added to dividing animal cells. Nuclei finish dividing, but many cells remain as one large cell containing two nuclei. Which conclusion is best supported? All other conditions are kept constant between groups.
choices: The result shows that cytokinesis always occurs before mitosis in animal cells.
The inhibitor caused a cell plate to form too early, trapping both nuclei in one compartment.
The inhibitor prevented DNA replication, which is why two nuclei formed in one cell.
The inhibitor blocked formation of the cleavage furrow, so mitosis occurred but cytokinesis was impaired.
questions.explain: In animal cells, actin and myosin help constrict the cleavage furrow during cytokinesis.
choice_explanations: The observation actually shows that nuclear division happened while cytokinesis failed, so cytokinesis does not always occur first.
Animal cells do not normally form a cell plate; that mechanism is typical of plant cytokinesis.
DNA replication occurs before mitosis and does not explain why nuclei divide but the cytoplasm does not separate.
Correct. In animal cells, actin and myosin help constrict the cleavage furrow during cytokinesis.

questions.text: Plant cells are treated with a compound that prevents vesicles from fusing at the center of the cell during division. Chromosomes separate normally, but daughter cells do not form a proper partition between them. Which interpretation is best supported? The measurements were repeated in three trials with the same overall trend.
choices: The treatment disrupted cell plate formation during cytokinesis in plant cells.
The treatment blocked the cleavage furrow, which is the main mechanism of plant cytokinesis.
The treatment prevented kinase signaling, so chromosomes could not separate during anaphase.
The partition failed because plant cells require centrioles to pinch inward during cytokinesis.
questions.explain: Plant cells build a cell plate from vesicles; blocking vesicle fusion interferes with cytokinesis.
choice_explanations: Correct. Plant cells build a cell plate from vesicles; blocking vesicle fusion interferes with cytokinesis.
This reverses the mechanisms. Plant cells use a cell plate, not a typical cleavage furrow, because the wall is rigid.
The prompt says chromosomes separated normally; the defect is in partitioning the cytoplasm afterward.
The main problem described is cell plate assembly from vesicles, not centriole-driven pinching.

questions.text: A bacterial population is grown in broth. At low cell density, a certain gene is expressed at a very low level. As the population becomes dense, the cells release and detect more of a small signaling molecule, and expression of the gene rises sharply in most cells at once. Which concept is best illustrated? All other conditions are kept constant between groups.
choices: ligand-gated ion channel signaling in neurons
endocrine signaling
DNA replication checkpoint control
quorum sensing
questions.explain: The coordinated response triggered by increasing signal concentration at high population density is characteristic of quorum sensing.
choice_explanations: The prompt describes coordinated bacterial signaling with population density, not synaptic ion-channel signaling.
Endocrine signaling refers to long-distance hormone signaling in multicellular organisms, not density-dependent bacterial signaling in a broth culture.
This experiment focuses on population-density communication and gene expression, not a eukaryotic cell-cycle checkpoint.
Correct. The coordinated response triggered by increasing signal concentration at high population density is characteristic of quorum sensing.

questions.text: Researchers grow two cell populations side by side but separate them with a membrane that allows dissolved molecules to pass while preventing the cells from touching. A response occurs only when the membrane is removed and the two cell types can make direct contact. Which conclusion is best supported? All other conditions are kept constant between groups.
choices: The signaling requires direct cell-cell contact, consistent with contact-dependent or juxtacrine signaling.
The response must be caused by quorum sensing, because the cells are nearby.
The signal is endocrine because only cells touching each other can release hormones into the bloodstream.
The membrane must have blocked all ions and therefore stopped DNA replication in both cell types.
questions.explain: If diffusible molecules can cross but the response appears only after direct contact, the signaling likely depends on membrane-bound components or direct contact.
choice_explanations: Correct. If diffusible molecules can cross but the response appears only after direct contact, the signaling likely depends on membrane-bound components or direct contact.
Quorum sensing involves diffusible signals reaching a threshold concentration. Here diffusible molecules could already cross, yet the response required actual contact.
Endocrine signaling involves long-distance hormones carried through circulatory fluid, not direct contact between neighboring cells.
The key comparison is the presence or absence of physical contact, not a generalized block of DNA replication.

questions.text: A researcher treats one set of cells with a drug suspected to trigger apoptosis and leaves another set untreated. After 4 hours, the treated cells show much higher caspase activity and DNA fragmentation, while membrane integrity remains largely intact early in the process. Which interpretation is most consistent with these observations? All other conditions are kept constant between groups.
choices: The drug is causing binary fission, because DNA is being processed and membrane integrity is still present.
The drug is forcing cells directly into S phase, which always increases caspase activity before DNA replication.
The drug is inducing apoptosis rather than immediate necrotic lysis.
The drug must be a growth factor, because DNA fragmentation shows that chromosomes are condensing for mitosis.
questions.explain: Caspase activation, DNA fragmentation, and orderly preservation of membrane integrity early on are strong signs of apoptosis.
choice_explanations: Binary fission is prokaryotic cell division, not a caspase-associated cell-death program in eukaryotic cells.
Caspase activity is associated with apoptosis, not a routine prerequisite for entry into S phase.
Correct. Caspase activation, DNA fragmentation, and orderly preservation of membrane integrity early on are strong signs of apoptosis.
DNA fragmentation and caspase activation indicate programmed cell death, not normal growth-factor stimulation or mitotic condensation.

questions.text: Scientists add testosterone to target cells and then add a transcription inhibitor. The hormone still enters the cells, but the long-term change in protein production largely disappears. Which conclusion is best supported? All other conditions are kept constant between groups.
choices: A major part of the hormone's effect depends on altering transcription after binding an intracellular receptor.
Because transcription changed, the hormone itself must be converted into mRNA inside the nucleus.
The hormone must act only through a ligand-gated ion channel on the membrane, because transcription is irrelevant to steroid-like signaling.
The inhibitor probably prevented the hormone from crossing the membrane, which is why protein production changed.
questions.explain: If the hormone enters cells but its long-term effect disappears when transcription is blocked, then gene regulation is an important part of the response.
choice_explanations: Correct. If the hormone enters cells but its long-term effect disappears when transcription is blocked, then gene regulation is an important part of the response.
Hormones regulate signaling pathways and gene expression, but they are not converted into mRNA.
This is the opposite of what the experiment suggests. The dependence on transcription fits intracellular receptor signaling, not an ion channel-only mechanism.
The prompt explicitly says the hormone still entered the cells, so membrane crossing is not the blocked step.

questions.text: In one cell line, a receptor binds its ligand normally but cannot activate the associated G protein. In a second cell line, a receptor tyrosine kinase binds ligand normally but cannot autophosphorylate. In both cases, downstream signaling is weak. Which general principle is best supported? All other conditions are kept constant between groups.
choices: A receptor cannot bind ligand unless its entire downstream pathway is already active first.
Transduction occurs only in the nucleus, so membrane-associated defects should not affect it.
Successful ligand binding alone is not enough; receptor activation must also be coupled to the correct transduction step.
All receptors use the same downstream molecules, so blocking any receptor should produce identical responses in every cell.
questions.explain: Both mutants can receive the signal at the binding step but fail to relay it effectively because the activation/transduction step is broken.
choice_explanations: The setups show the opposite: ligand binding still happens even though downstream signaling is weak.
Signal transduction commonly begins at the membrane or in the cytoplasm. Defects in receptor activation can strongly disrupt it.
Correct. Both mutants can receive the signal at the binding step but fail to relay it effectively because the activation/transduction step is broken.
Different receptors can use different pathways and responses. The shared principle here is that binding without activation is insufficient, not that all pathways are identical.
